data.description:
# Optional authentication for the rebar MCP server (OAuth 2.1 Resource-Server model)

## Context

`rebar-mcp` (`src/rebar/mcp_server.py`, `mcp.server.fastmcp.FastMCP("rebar")`) is today
**stdio-only**: `build_server().run()` is called with no transport argument, so it speaks
MCP-over-stdio to a locally-spawned subprocess. It has **no HTTP transport, no network bind,
and no per-request/session/header plumbing**. Per the MCP authorization spec, authentication
is an **HTTP-transport concern** — stdio servers *should not* authenticate (credentials come
from the environment). rebar has deliberately kept MCP stdio-only and met every remote need
with separate FastAPI library-mode services (the Gerrit `review_bot`, the in-flight op-cert
service), so **nothing consumes rebar-mcp over the network today** (Gerrit and GitHub Actions
use the rebar library/CLI in-process, per ADR-0007).

This epic adds an **optional** capability: rebar-mcp can serve over HTTP and require
authentication, acting as an **OAuth 2.1 Resource Server** that validates inbound bearer
tokens and advertises RFC 9728 Protected Resource Metadata. The capability is generic
(provider-agnostic) so any organization deploying rebar can front it with its own identity
provider — it is **not** coupled to this project's own dev auth model.

Design-of-record: session log `emanatory-confounded-dodo` (grounded investigation + the
confirmed decisions + the security posture + the SDK API surface + prior-art survey).

## Why (beneficiary + problem)

**Beneficiary:** an operator/organization (including rebar itself, as future follow-on) that
wants to expose the rebar MCP tools to remote MCP clients (e.g. Claude Code / an IDE / a CI
job) over the network instead of a local stdio subprocess. **Problem today:** there is no way
to do this at all — no HTTP transport and no authentication — so a shared/hosted rebar store
cannot be reached by a remote MCP client without exposing it unauthenticated. This epic makes
a remote rebar-mcp endpoint *possible and safe to require auth on*, verified locally.

## Approach & rationale (positive)

Build rebar's **own** token verifiers against the **official `mcp` SDK's `TokenVerifier`
seam** (`mcp.server.auth.provider.TokenVerifier.verify_token(token) -> AccessToken | None`),
wired via `FastMCP(token_verifier=…, auth=AuthSettings(…))`, and serve the optional
Streamable-HTTP transport the SDK already provides. rebar is **a Resource Server only** — it
validates tokens and advertises discovery metadata; it is **not** an Authorization Server and
does **not** implement Dynamic Client Registration (identity is delegated to the deploying
org's IdP).

**Why this approach fits:** (a) the official SDK — already a rebar dependency — provides the
`TokenVerifier` seam, `AuthSettings`, the RFC 9728 metadata routes, and the Streamable-HTTP
transport natively, so the verifiers are ~50-100 LOC each on a stable, blessed seam; (b) it is
the mainstream path (GitHub, Grafana, Neon, GitLab, Sentry, and the SDK's own `simple-auth`
example all build auth on the official SDK / a gateway, not the standalone `fastmcp`
framework); (c) it avoids adopting the standalone `fastmcp` package, whose `server.auth` module
is explicitly exempt from semantic versioning (may break on patch releases; has had CVEs) and
which would pull ~15 extra transitive libraries; (d) it keeps every line under rebar's own
tests, CI, and version control, matching rebar's minimal-dependency, own-the-code ethos and
module-size policy.

Prior art / alternatives considered are recorded in the design-of-record session log:
standalone `fastmcp` (rejected — heavy + unstable auth surface) and edge-proxy-only auth
(supported as one verifier mode, but rebar still provides in-app auth per the generic-capability
requirement).

## Security (this epic crosses a trust boundary — read this)

Enabling HTTP exposes rebar-mcp to **lower-trust network actors**, so the security posture is
first-class and every sub-item below is a hard requirement propagated to the children:

- **Authentication is the deliverable.** With auth enabled, every request is admitted only on a
  validated bearer token; an unauthenticated request receives **401 + `WWW-Authenticate`** with
  the RFC 9728 `resource_metadata` URL. Human/admin identity is delegated to the deploying org's
  IdP (via the JWKS/introspection/trusted-proxy verifiers); rebar mints no tokens.
- **Audience validation is mandatory in every verifier** — the SDK does **not** validate token
  audience/resource, so each verifier validates `aud`/resource itself (equivalent to always-on
  `--oauth-strict`, RFC 8707). A token issued for another service is rejected.
- **Fail-closed everywhere:** any verifier error denies; misconfigured auth refuses to start;
  an HTTP transport with auth **disabled** refuses to start unless an explicit
  `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_UNAUTHENTICATED_HTTP` acknowledgment is set (no accidental open endpoint).
- **Transport hardening:** DNS-rebinding / `Origin` protection on by default
  (`TransportSecuritySettings`), loopback bind by default, explicit host/origin allowlist
  required for a non-loopback bind (avoids the SDK's historical default-off DNS-rebinding CVE
  GHSA-9h52-p55h-vw2f).
- **No token passthrough** to any upstream (confused-deputy prohibition); TLS is assumed
  terminated at the edge (bearer tokens never in query strings, never logged).
- Per-verifier hardening (algorithm pinning, SSRF-safe JWKS fetch, constant-time compare +
  hash-at-rest, introspection fail-closed, trusted-proxy shared-secret + header stripping) is
  specified in the child stories.

## Scope

**In scope:** an optional Streamable-HTTP transport for rebar-mcp; a provider-agnostic auth
framework (composite `TokenVerifier` + config-driven selection) shipping five verifiers (static
bearer, JWKS/JWT, RFC 7662 introspection, trusted-proxy passthrough, pluggable custom); RFC 9728
metadata + 401 challenge; the security/transport hardening above; local verification (pytest);
docs + an ADR; config/manifest updates (`McpConfig`, `MCP_ENV_VARS`, `server.json`, regenerated
`mcp-reference.md`/`env-vars.md`).

**Out of scope (explicit):** deploying an authenticated endpoint on AWS /
`rebar.solutions.navateam.com` and any Gerrit/CI reconfiguration (Gerrit and CI do **not** use
MCP — this is deliberate follow-on); rebar acting as an Authorization Server / Dynamic Client
Registration / issuing tokens; per-tool scope→capability authorization (server-level
`required_scopes` only); GitHub-**user** authentication and Gerrit authentication (neither is an
OAuth token issuer — only ever a deploying org's proxy/custom-verifier concern). stdio remains
the default transport and is unchanged.

## Success Criteria

- A rebar-mcp operator can run the server over HTTP with authentication **required**, using any
  of the five shipped verifier modes, configured entirely through the existing `[mcp]`
  config/env surface — with stdio still the default and auth off by default.
- With auth enabled, the trust-boundary guarantees hold and are demonstrated locally: an
  unauthenticated request is rejected (401 + `WWW-Authenticate` + PRM), a valid token is
  accepted (200), an insufficient-scope token is rejected (403), a wrong-audience token is
  rejected, and DNS-rebinding/Origin protection rejects a disallowed `Host`/`Origin` (421/403).
- An HTTP transport cannot be started unauthenticated without an explicit acknowledgment.
- The capability is provider-agnostic (no rebar-specific IdP assumptions) and documented for a
  third-party operator.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] All child stories (S1–S7) are closed.
- [ ] `rebar-mcp` can serve over an optional Streamable-HTTP transport while stdio remains the
      default and default behavior is byte-unchanged when the new config keys are unset
      (observable: the existing `tests/interfaces/facades/test_mcp.py` stdio suite still passes).
- [ ] With auth enabled, the local test suite demonstrates 401-without-token (with a
      `WWW-Authenticate` header carrying the RFC 9728 `resource_metadata` URL), a served PRM
      document, 200-with-valid-token, 403-insufficient-scope, and wrong-audience rejection.
- [ ] All five verifier modes (static, JWKS/JWT, introspection, trusted-proxy, custom) are
      selectable via `[mcp.auth]` config / `REBAR_MCP_*` env vars, and each has passing tests.
- [ ] Starting an HTTP transport with auth disabled fails fast unless
      `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_UNAUTHENTICATED_HTTP` is set (test asserts the refusal).
- [ ] New config keys and env vars are reflected in `MCP_ENV_VARS` and `server.json` (the CI
      manifest drift gate `scripts/check_server_manifest.py` passes) and in regenerated
      `docs/mcp-reference.md` / `docs/env-vars.md`.
- [ ] `docs/mcp-auth.md` and an ADR document the resource-server-only model, the five verifier
      modes, the security posture, and behind-a-proxy deployment guidance.
- [ ] `make lint && make typecheck && make test` pass on the final integrated branch.

## Testing strategy (children carry the tiers/oracles)

Verification is entirely local (no AWS). Each verifier is unit-tested against its own real
logic (valid/invalid/audience/expiry/algorithm-confusion/rotation/fail-closed/header-spoofing
cases), and the HTTP+auth surface is integration-tested by driving `mcp.streamable_http_app()`
with an `httpx` `ASGITransport` client (asserting HTTP status, the `WWW-Authenticate` header,
and the served PRM document). Each child story names its test tier and observable oracle.

## Compatibility & rollback

Purely **additive**: stdio stays the default transport, auth is **off by default**, and all
new behavior is gated behind new, unset-by-default `[mcp]` config keys, so existing stdio
deployments and the existing MCP tests are unaffected. The one dependency change is pinning
`mcp>=1.28,<2` (the v2-alpha renames the SDK surface) and adding `pyjwt[crypto]` for the JWKS
verifier. Rollback is config-level: leave the new keys unset (or `mcp.transport=stdio`) to get
exactly today's behavior.
data.title: Optional authentication for the rebar MCP server (OAuth 2.1 Resource-Server model)
